cards.back: Compiler extensions are features added by specific compilers (MSVC, GCC, Clang) that are not part of the official C++ standard. They provide extra functionality but hurt portability.

cards.text: Compiler extensions are {{c1::compiler-specific}} features that are {{c2::not}} part of the official C++ standard.

cards.front: What is the main problem with using compiler extensions?
cards.back: Code using compiler extensions won't compile on other compilers. This hurts portability - your code becomes locked to one compiler vendor.

cards.concept: Why you should disable compiler extensions
cards.explanation:
Disabling compiler extensions is a best practice because:
  • Makes code portable across different compilers
  • Ensures code follows the C++ standard
  • Prevents accidental use of non-standard features
  • Makes it easier to switch compilers later
  • Code is more likely to work in future C++ versions
Extensions may seem convenient but create technical debt.
examples: GCC extension: variable-length arrays (not in standard C++)
MSVC extension: non-standard string literal behavior
Code with extensions: works only on one compiler

cards.text: To ensure maximum portability, you should {{c1::disable}} compiler extensions in your project settings.

cards.front: How do you disable compiler extensions in Visual Studio?
cards.back: Project Properties → C/C++ → Language → Conformance mode → set to Yes (/permissive-)

cards.front: How do you disable compiler extensions in GCC and Clang?
cards.back: Use the -pedantic-errors flag when compiling. This treats extension usage as errors.

cards.text: In GCC/Clang, the {{c1::-pedantic-errors}} flag disables compiler extensions and treats their use as {{c2::errors}}.

cards.front: What is "conformance mode" in Visual Studio?
cards.back: Conformance mode (/permissive-) makes MSVC strictly follow the C++ standard and disable most Microsoft-specific extensions, improving code portability.

cards.front: When might you intentionally use compiler extensions?
cards.back: Only when absolutely necessary for platform-specific functionality (OS APIs, hardware access) and when portability isn't a concern. Always isolate extension use to specific files.

cards.concept: Setting up a new C++ project for standards compliance
cards.explanation:
Configure every new project for maximum standards compliance:
  • Disable compiler extensions (conformance mode)
  • Enable all warnings
  • Treat warnings as errors
  • Choose a specific C++ standard (C++20, C++23)
  • Test on multiple compilers if possible
These settings catch problems early and ensure portable code.
